title: Quiz: Circuit Complexity Metrics
description: Test your understanding of quantum circuit complexity metrics and their importance for hardware execution

questions.question: What does circuit depth represent in a quantum circuit?

options.text: The total number of gates in the circuit
The number of qubits used by the circuit
The number of time steps required to execute the circuit
The memory required to simulate the circuit

questions.explanation: Circuit depth is the number of time steps (or layers) required to execute the circuit, where gates that can be executed in parallel count as a single time step. It represents the critical path length and directly impacts decoherence.

questions.question: For a circuit with gates that have fidelities F_1Q = 0.999 for single-qubit and F_2Q = 0.99 for two-qubit gates, with 100 single-qubit and 50 two-qubit gates, what is the approximate circuit fidelity?
questions.explanation: Circuit fidelity is estimated as F_circuit ≈ F_1Q^(N_1Q) × F_2Q^(N_2Q). With 100 single-qubit gates and 50 two-qubit gates: 0.999^100 × 0.99^50 ≈ 0.905 × 0.605 ≈ 0.55. The two-qubit gates dominate the error.

questions.question: Why is the T-count metric particularly important for fault-tolerant quantum computing?

options.text: T gates are the slowest gates to execute
T gates require expensive magic state distillation while Clifford gates can be implemented transversally
T gates use the most qubits
T gates cannot be parallelized

questions.explanation: In fault-tolerant quantum computing with error-correcting codes, Clifford gates (H, S, CNOT) can be implemented transversally (fault-tolerantly on logical qubits), but T gates require costly magic state distillation. This makes T-count the dominant resource for fault-tolerant algorithms.

questions.question: A circuit has 8 qubits and depth 20. What is its circuit volume?
questions.explanation: Circuit volume V = width × depth = n × D = 8 × 20 = 160. This represents the total spacetime resources required by the circuit.

questions.question: Two-qubit gates are typically 10-100x more error-prone than single-qubit gates on real quantum hardware.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. On current superconducting and trapped-ion hardware, two-qubit gate fidelities are typically around 99-99.5%, while single-qubit gate fidelities are around 99.9-99.99%. This makes the two-qubit gate count a critical predictor of circuit success.

questions.question: What does connectivity density measure in circuit analysis?

options.text: The ratio of gates to qubits
The ratio of unique interacting qubit pairs to maximum possible pairs
The number of SWAP gates needed
The physical distance between qubits on the chip

questions.explanation: Connectivity density is the ratio of unique qubit pairs that actually interact in the circuit to the maximum possible pairs (n(n-1)/2 for n qubits). High connectivity density may indicate significant SWAP overhead when mapping to hardware with limited connectivity.

questions.question: For a GHZ state preparation circuit on n qubits (one H gate followed by n-1 CNOT gates in a chain), what is the circuit depth?

options.text: n

questions.explanation: The GHZ circuit has depth n: one layer for the H gate on qubit 0, then (n-1) layers for the sequential CNOT gates (CX(0,1), CX(1,2), ...). These CNOTs cannot be parallelized as each depends on the previous one completing.

questions.question: Which of the following are reasons to profile circuits before hardware execution? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Circuits may exceed the coherence time limit
Gate counts affect expected fidelity
Connectivity requirements may cause SWAP overhead
To determine the classical algorithm complexity

questions.explanation: Profiling reveals: (a) whether depth exceeds coherence limits, (b) expected fidelity based on gate counts and error rates, and (c) connectivity mismatches that require SWAP insertions. Option (d) is incorrect as circuit metrics measure quantum, not classical, complexity.
